# Transcrição: 09-09  What's Working Now on FB & Native

- **Fonte:** 09-09  What's Working Now on FB & Native
- **Duração:** 35:00
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

So, I thought I would start by telling you guys a little bit about my background and how we end up from ClickBank to Holy Land to kids books. So in 2015, I started buying 10 cent desktop clicks on Yahoo Gemini. And very quickly became the largest paid advertiser on Yahoo! running zero Facebook. The only reason I know that we were the largest advertiser on Yahoo! was because one of my media buyers got an ad with a scotch glass and a headline that said, how diabetics are having more sex. And so we called around and we got an E.D. offer that we put on the back end, which you can't run. And we were making, I think that day we made $30,000 in gross profit. But we already knew in Yahoo! that you had to bank backup ads. So when they took it down, we ran it again and Marissa Mayer called some guy who called us on a Saturday and we got in trouble. You're going to hear a lot about getting in trouble. We've done that quite a bit. And so we started running a bunch of these ClickBank type offers, super aggressive. We were making a lot of money. It was great. Got introduced to Stefan with probably one of the worst offers I've ever seen in my entire life. And we got begged to run it and we ran it and that thing just was fire. And Stefan and I were off to the races. And we'd had business managers swept kind of several times and we just started to get a lot of fatigue. And then when Facebook came out with their whole Cambridge Analytica stuff, the headache just got way too difficult. And so we really thought about what's the most white hat of white hat offers you can think of and we made a kid's book. And we sold about 5,000 copies in 2017, basically broke even and said, oh my gosh, we're kind of onto something. And the reason I bring that up is because as I give you our perspective on Facebook, it really sort of straddles two sides of the industry. One is very e-comy, brandy. I mean the company's called Puppy Dogs and Ice Cream. And the way that we came up with that name was when we used to pitch clients, I used to tell them, I'm like, look, we don't sell Puppy Dogs, Ice Cream, Hula Hoops, Rainbows, or feelings. If you have a brand and care about it, don't roam with us. If you want sales, then you come to us. So when we needed something with a brand, we decided to do that. And we've had a tremendous amount of success. This is our new hero product, Fiona Flamingo. I looked yesterday. It is the number 268th, which was kind of down, but out of all the books on Amazon today. And we're running zero traffic to Amazon. If that gives you a sense of how many people go through, break a funnel and then still go buy on Amazon. So I think the biggest change in Facebook that's coming today is Facebook is actually getting dumber. So as they start to remove all the data points that they used to have, they used to be able to just target diabetics. And that just made it really easily easy to target.

## [03:00 - 06:03]

And so as they've pulled off kind of quarter after quarter, year after year, the big change is that you've got to run broad audiences. You've got to run really, really wide. And so where we used to run like, one in 2% lookalikes at one point for Holy Land, we were running $1,000 a day per ad account and 250 ad accounts just crushing. That doesn't work. I actually ran a test of that about three, four months ago, lost my ass. But now I know that it doesn't work. So as you start, you basically just want to scale really, really slowly. And that's one of the things that's worked quite well for us. Now we start with higher budgets than I would say most companies do. We don't start at $20 ad set budgets or anything along those lines. You just push 20% a day, 20% a day, and you scale up until you get to a number that makes sense. And we also hear this from Facebook quite a bit now, where they've returned to a creative first platform. Basically, I view that as them telling us that their algorithm simply isn't as good as it once was. However, Facebook as a traffic source is generally very good. My belief around that and why I think it beats Instagram and a bunch of the story traffic is Facebook replaces television. If you want to sit and engage for a longer duration of time, Facebook traffic is very good. Instagram is what you do with the bar for five minutes while you're waiting for your buddy to meet you. This was an interesting thing. I had a conversation with Robbie at Trident True Media about three months ago around the idea of retargeting. He's like, dude, does retargeting work for you guys? I was like, no. I have tried retargeting every single time with every single offer, and I cannot get it to work. It's me too. I don't know what the problem is. The big issue for us is that when primarily we ran retargeting to offers that we did not own, Facebook automatically will retarget about 20% of your traffic anyway. If you're dropping $20,000, $30,000, $50,000 a day on an offer, Facebook is automatically going to retarget that audience. Their algorithm is what I would call that smart, not brilliant with the same metrics that they used to have. Then when we moved into the kids' book space, for once we finally owned the offer, and we could run SMS and Google AdWords, and we could run the card abandonments, and we could run different steps to the funnel, browse, because we have an e-com store, all of a sudden retargeting started to work. Then we started playing with the view content, initiate checkout, add to cart, and all those types of different steps. Then from there, we started testing different objectives. One of the big tests that we've been doing that has been yielding pretty good results so far is running an add to cart objective to really rebuild your retargeting around the add to cart initiate checkout audience. If you're not doing that, I suggest doing it. You can't run it at scale. I've tried that. The algorithm is still smart. Same thing with running to a payment objective instead of a purchase. You spend enough money, and then Facebook will start to optimize to people who put in their credit cards. Yes, if you're running a Shopify or two-step cart, you still get those emails and you can retarget them, but better to close them on the spot.

## [06:04 - 09:07]

Here are a couple of things that I'm thankful for today. The first one is minimum ROAS. If you guys aren't running MinROAS, I highly suggest it. It yields great results, but the metrics are wildly different. I don't know how Facebook is calculating those users on MinROAS. Your CPMs will be dramatically higher, but your ROAS is also higher. I probably ask our rep every single week on a weekly call about how the hell this works and how do we set it up and how do you configure, and she has no clue. But it's definitely worth testing. We've also found that it works a lot better on offers that are getting a lot of traffic and a high number of conversions, and it also works on products that have a lower ticket price. Oh, ROAS is return on ad spend. And that's kind of what we used to manage. The return on ad spend. In terms of CBO, it's called conversion budget optimum. Wait, what does it call? I forget what it's called. Right. Campaign budget optimization. And so the way that Facebook used to run is that you would have a campaign, then you have your ad sets, which is your targeting, and then you would adjust your budgets for each one of those ad sets. And then Facebook said, we're smarter than you. And so we're going to move that optimization back up to the campaign level. We're going to optimize the target and allocate the spend the way that we want in the ad set level, which basically just takes away one of the levers from a media buyer, which it kind of sucks, right? Like Facebook's great, but you want to have as much control over your traffic as you can.

## [15:11 - 18:12]

We're running a tremendous amount of volume on that offer that does have before and afters. We lost one of our main spend accounts, what day is today? Today's Friday. We lost it on Wednesday. We got it back on Thursday. We've lost that account three times, gotten it back. I don't know how many hundreds of thousands of dollars we have on that one ad account. But it works. One of the questions that comes up also is, how do I know where my ad sits in the feed? The higher up the feed you sit, the better your ad does, right? You have less ad fatigue. And the way that they calculate where your ad sits is based on total value, which is the bid. So if you're an auto, it doesn't really matter. Estimated auction, so that's the conversion rate. How well does your offer convert? That tells Facebook how well people are receiving that offer. And then user value, which is the soft metric that you kind of don't really know what that means. One of the other new things is they're telling us that engagement does not matter. I'm not sure I believe this. A lot of our ads get a tremendous amount of engagement. But I thought that that was really interesting in the sense that you don't necessarily outside of social proof have to warm up your posts anymore to get page likes or to get ad likes and shares.
